nativeRange.habitat: Calm or slow-moving freshwater — ponds, oxbow lakes, ditches, swamps, marshes and the quiet margins of slow tropical rivers; floats freely on the surface, with the leaf rosette held above the water and a dense beard of feathery roots dangling beneath

decorPreferences: open, calm water surface — avoid spray bars, strong surface returns, and persistent splashing
open-top or rim-only lid so the rosettes can sit above the waterline; heavy condensation on a closed lid causes the velvety leaves to rot
floating ring or corral if the mat needs to be kept away from filter inlets or one side of the tank
medium-to-high ambient humidity around the leaves

goodWith: live-bearing fish fry (provides dense shade and shelter via dangling roots)
shrimp (browse biofilm on the root mass)
labyrinth fish (gouramis, bettas) that use surface cover for bubble nests
outdoor ponds in summer in temperate climates
avoidWith: goldfish and other vigorous plant-eating cyprinids
tanks with strong surface agitation, spray bars, or persistent overhead drip that wets the leaves
closed-lid tanks with heavy condensation on the cover glass

summary: Water Lettuce (Pistia stratiotes) is a pan-tropical floating plant that forms a rosette of velvety, light-green leaves above the waterline and a long beard of feathery roots below. Provides excellent shade, fry cover and nutrient export, but is a regulated noxious weed in many jurisdictions.
careNotes: Pistia stratiotes is a monotypic aroid that floats as a rosette of soft, light-green, parallel-veined leaves coated in fine water-repellent hairs, with a dense plume of feathery roots dangling beneath. The leaves are tender — persistent wetting from spray bars, lid condensation, or splashing filter returns will cause them to melt or brown — so the species is best suited to open-top tanks (or tanks with at least a generous air gap above the water) and gentle surface flow. Lighting is forgiving in the aquarium hobby sense, but Modern Aquarium notes that stronger light yields tighter, more compact rosettes and faster growth; CO2 injection is not required and fertilization can stay light since the plant pulls nutrients directly from the water column. Wikipedia gives an optimum temperature window of 22-30 °C (tolerant up to ~35 °C, intolerant of cold below the tropics of Cancer/Capricorn) and an optimum pH of 6.5-7.2; Modern Aquarium's 22-28 °C (72-82 °F) sits inside that window, so the wider Wikipedia range is used. Hardness is not documented in either source — most aquarium-hobby reports treat the species as adaptable across typical freshwater hardness; left null. Growth rate is fast under warm conditions; expect to thin the mat weekly to keep the surface from sealing over and starving submerged plants of light. The roots make outstanding fry cover and shrimp grazing surface. INVASIVE / LEGAL NOTE: Pistia stratiotes is a U.S. Federal Noxious Weed and is banned or restricted in many U.S. states (Florida, Texas, South Carolina, Mississippi and others), in Australia, in the EU (Union list since August 2024), and in several African and Asian countries; check local regulations before purchase, and never release into natural waterways. Cross-source verification: Wikipedia (primary) and Modern Aquarium (secondary) agreed on growth form (floating rosette, velvety leaves, dangling roots), warm tropical temperature preference, floating placement, and propagation via stolons/daughter plants; designated tertiaries Buce Plant (404) and Aquarium Co-Op (no species-specific care page surfaced) were unavailable, so the entry rests on two-source confirmation.
breedingNotes: Reproduces overwhelmingly by vegetative propagation in aquaria: healthy parent rosettes throw off daughter plantlets on short stolons, which detach and form independent rosettes — this is how a small purchase rapidly fills the surface of a tank or pond. Plants are dioecious and can also produce small, petal-less flowers hidden in the centre of the rosette, leading to oval green berries containing ovoid seeds; sexual reproduction is occasional in cultivation and not relied upon. Vegetative spread is so prolific that it underpins the species' invasive status — even small fragments can re-establish, so all trimmings should be bagged and binned, never composted into a waterway.

primary.name: Wikipedia
primary.notes: Provided taxonomy (Araceae, Alismatales, monotypic Pistia), pan-tropical native range (Africa, South America, southeastern US), growth form (perennial floating rosette, velvety hair-covered leaves 2-15 cm long, dangling roots), optimum temperature 22-30 °C (tolerant to 35 °C), optimum pH 6.5-7.2, propagation via stolons (vegetative) plus dioecious flowers and seeds, and invasive / regulatory status (EU ban from August 2024; IUCN Least Concern but problematic globally).

additional.name: Modern Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked aquarium-husbandry side: temperature 72-82 °F (22-28 °C — within Wikipedia's 22-30 °C window), placement floating, care level easy, lighting moderate-to-strong (stronger light → tighter rosettes, faster growth), CO2 not required, fertilization via water column / all-in-one liquid ferts, fast growth and easy vegetative propagation via daughter plants, and the surface-agitation warning (avoid strong agitation that flips rosettes; keep lid open or with generous air gap; medium-to-high humidity preferred; lid condensation causes melt).

additional.name: Buce Plant
additional.notes: Attempted as tertiary cross-check per the slice fallback (Wikipedia → Modern Aquarium → Buce Plant → Aquarium Co-Op). Returned HTTP 404 — the product page no longer exists, likely because Buce Plant cannot ship Pistia stratiotes to many US states under noxious-weed restrictions. Not used.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Attempted as fourth fallback after Buce Plant 404. The /products/water-lettuce URL did not surface a species-specific care page, and the broader floating-plants blog index did not contain Pistia stratiotes care details in the fetched content. Not used for confirmation.

propagation: vegetative — daughter plantlets on stolons (primary in aquaria)
occasional sexual reproduction via dioecious flowers and seeds (rare in cultivation)

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Pistia stratiotes is a U.S. Federal Noxious Weed and is prohibited or restricted in many U.S. states (e.g. Florida, Texas, South Carolina, Mississippi), in Australia, across the EU (Union list of invasive alien species of concern since August 2024), and in several African and Asian countries. Aquarists must verify local regulations before purchase and must never release fragments into natural waterways.
